entries.title: Quiet Hours
entries.body:
Dear Nicole,

It's six in the morning and the kettle is doing its small operatic thing on the stove. I have been awake for an hour because I wanted to be the one to hear the day first today, which is a strange ambition but feels like the right one.

You are still asleep in the next room, and from here I can see the bend of your shoulder and the part of the duvet you always claim is yours. You are wrong about that, of course. The duvet is communal. I will keep losing this argument graciously.

Three years. I keep wanting to find some new way to say it but the only honest version is the boring one — *it has gone fast and I am grateful*. That's it. That's the whole sentence.

I made coffee. It is too strong. You will complain. I will pretend to be sorry.

Happy anniversary.

m.

entries.title: The Thing About Tuesdays
entries.note: Recorded after the fact — close to verbatim.
entries.body:
I told you, sitting on the kitchen floor, that I have always been a little afraid of Tuesdays. Not afraid like a phobia. Afraid like Tuesdays are the day where you find out whether the week is going to be a good week or a hard one.

You laughed and said that was the most unhinged thing you'd heard all month, and then a minute later you said: *but yes, actually, that's true, isn't it.*

That's a thing I love about you. The arriving at agreement only after the laughing.

I think I also said — and you can correct me on this — that I'd rather have a hard Tuesday with you than an easy one without. Which sounds like the kind of thing people put on a mug. But I meant it then and I mean it now, and I will keep meaning it on the harder Tuesdays especially.

entries.title: Not a Valentine
entries.note: Probably I won't send this one.
entries.body:
There is a thing I have not said to you, and the reason I have not said it is that I think you already know, and saying it would feel like reaching for the light switch in a room that's already lit.

But just in case.

I think you are the most attentive person I have ever met. You notice the temperature of a room as soon as you enter it. You notice when I have been pretending to listen to a podcast and you notice when I am actually listening to one. You notice the way the woman at the corner shop is having a bad week, and you remember her name, and you ask after the dog.

I used to think attention was a small thing. It is not a small thing. It might be the largest thing.

If you are reading this, then I gave it to you after all, which means I was braver than I thought I would be, and you should know that you are the reason for that.

Not a valentine.

— m.

entries.title: Second Year
entries.note: Written from the kitchen, two years in.
entries.body: Two years. The flat smells like the bread you started yesterday. I have read the same paragraph four times and I keep losing it to the sound of you humming. I think that's the whole report. I think that's the year.

entries.title: The Day After
entries.body: I forgot to say it yesterday so I am saying it today: the best part of the anniversary was the bit afterwards, where nothing was special, and we just made dinner. That's the actual measurement. I think we are doing well.

entries.title: First Anniversary
entries.note: One year. Still figuring out the format.
entries.body: A year ago today we were eating cold noodles on the floor because the table hadn't arrived. I keep thinking about that, the way the boxes looked, the way you laughed. We have a table now. The laugh is the same. That's the part I wanted on record.

entries.title: A Half Year
entries.note: Not quite an anniversary. Counting anyway.
entries.body: Six months in and I am keeping a small list of the things you do that I did not expect to love. Top of the list this week: the way you read the back of the cereal box out loud, in a voice meant for theatre.

entries.title: Three Months
entries.body: Three months. I keep waiting for the part where I get tired of how you laugh in your sleep. The part has not arrived. I am beginning to suspect it isn't on the schedule. I will keep you posted.

entries.title: One Month
entries.body: One month is a silly thing to mark and I am marking it anyway. You left a mug in the sink with a tea bag still in it and I felt nothing but tenderness about it, which is, I think, the diagnosis.
